taskVersion.name: epu_q2_short
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_short")
def epu_q2_short(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, short_pre, short_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

Archi

TARGET ARTICLE START
Ltoking for orientation as the vast aesthetic whirlwind of Art Basel descends on Miami, we asked a half-dozen art world players what they consider the most compelling trends today. Their short answer: There are none. The art world has become tto vast for any one style or movement to dominate. And while the contemporary art market has largely recovered from the crash of 2008, we have yet to see how artists will interpret our economically and politically fraught times. • Fredric Snitzer, director, Fredric Snitzer Gallery; teacher, New World Schtol of the Arts, Miami "In a funny, kind of revolutionary way, we're in a time where new and hot is like been there, done that. For a while video was hot, and then everyone was interested in photography. Performance has come in and out. All these things have been absorbed into what's happening. "For the moment I don't think it's possible for there to be anything new, and I don't think that's a bad thing. Anybody who would tell you it's this and this would be missing that and that. All bets are off. In the old days you had movements. I can't remember the last time you had a movement. It's not possible. "I'm surprised there's not more political content or unrest in the work. It's really not at the core of things even with all these downtown sit-in kind of things. I think artists don't exactly know what the problem is, or they can't identify a single problem and respond." Shamim M. Momin, director/curator at LAND (Los Angeles Nomadic Divisio

[...TRUNCATED...]

in buying blue-chip work. "Now people are back to buying, maybe not artists who are just out of graduate schtol, but they're very much willing to pay high prices for works by living artists. I would qualify that by saying that the market is very selective. People are not going around buying brand names. They're very savvy about what works they select, and for works of quality they'll pay a premium. "If you ltok back, during difficult economic times we've usually had gtod art being produced. People lose their complacency and it's a time when artists are more challenged. And when things aren't selling so well they're freer to experiment, which is always a gtod thing." • Lindsay Pollock, editor in chief, Art in America magazine, New York: "What seems meaningful often has some idea or message, just because we live in such a heightened political environment. We particularly feel it in New York with Occupy Wall Street. People are questioning hierarchy and the status quo, although it hasn't flowed through to the artists yet. … "[Art Basel] is a trade show, an opportunity to buy and sell, but it will have its cerebral element. People are able to get this overview when the art world is growing more globalized. There is a takeaway, and at the end some things will stand out. "Hopefully you make a connection to galleries and go offline, put on your shoes and go to the gallery. That's what everyone hopes, that this keeps people interested in the art world."
TARGET ARTICLE END

Archiv
